← all tutorials

Linearizability — interactive 📖 LINEARIZABILITY.md 📄 linearizability.py

📖 Full guide  ·  📄 Ground-truth source — every panel below recomputes the same model in JS.
Every operation appears to execute atomically at one instant (the linearization point) between its invocation and response, and those points respect real-time order. Pick a history, drag the blue dots to place linearization points, and watch the verdict go green (linearizable) or red (violation). The same four histories are recomputed live from the identical data as linearizability.py.

① Client timeline — invocation/response intervals & real-time order

What to do: pick a history. Each bar spans an operation's [inv, resp] interval. A gold arrow means real-time order is forced (resp(A) ≤ inv(B) ⟹ A before B). Overlapping bars (no arrow between them) are concurrent and may be reordered freely. Default is HIST_B — the stale read that breaks linearizability.
write interval read interval real-time order (forced) initial X = 0

② Linearization points — drag the dots to the instant each op "takes effect"

What to do: drag each blue dot along its bar — that dot is the linearization point (the instant the op appears to execute). The replay sequence below recomputes instantly. Dots turn green when the whole story is a legal register history that respects real time; red when a read returns the wrong value or real-time order is violated.
drag a dot…

③ The checker — Wing & Gong: enumerate real-time orderings, test each

What this shows: the checker enumerates every total order consistent with real time (topological sorts) and replays each as a register. If any is a legal sequential history ⟹ linearizable. Toggle the model: linearizability (respects real time) vs sequential consistency (drops real time — weaker). HIST_B flips from NO to YES.

④ Implementation cost — linearizability needs consensus

Why it isn't free: guaranteeing every read sees the latest write forces replicas to coordinate (consensus / quorum) before responding. Under a network partition a linearizable store must refuse requests it cannot keep consistent — it is CP, not AP (Gilbert & Lynch 2002).
check: … JS checker matches linearizability.py: HIST_A=YES, HIST_B=NO, HIST_C=YES, HIST_D=YES; HIST_B is sequentially consistent.
